coredata.dc:title: Dual-excitation red-emissive carbon dots excited by ultraviolet light for the mitochondria-targetable imaging and monitoring of biological process in living cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

Han, Lifeng
Tian, Fei
coredata.dc:description:
The bifunctional carbon dots (CA-p-CDs) with truly red-emission under short-wavelength light excitation (such as ultraviolet light, blue light or cyan light) were easily and successfully fabricated by attaching citric acid (CA) molecules onto the pristine p-CDs using an ultrasonic-triggered surface re-engineering approach under room conditions. Especially, the CA-p-CDs show truly red emission and excitation-wavelength-independent properties when excited by ultraviolet-light with a wavelength in the range of 360–400 nm or excited by progressively longer excitation wavelengths in the range of 440–500 nm. The CA-p-CDs were verified to be very low cytotoxicity. Importantly, the CA-p-CDs were further used for the targeted mitochondria imaging or direct evaluation on the intracellular biological process in living cells through variable PL emission colors with the gradually depletion of attached CA molecules. Furthermore, the strongest PL peaks of CA-p-CDs showed fluorescence enhancement response toward Cr3+ ions. The CA-p-CDs were also used for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Cr3+ ions in tap water with a limit of detection as low as 12
nM
. This work presented designable fabrication of bifunctional CDs by linking molecules as an excellent nanoprobes for the mitochondria-targetable imaging and chemical sensing of heavy metal ions in water.

head.ce:title: Dual-excitation red-emissive carbon dots excited by ultraviolet light for the mitochondria-targetable imaging and monitoring of biological process in living cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthetic route CA-p-CDs and their application for mitochondria targetable imaging.

The bifunctional carbon dots (CA-p-CDs) with truly red-emission under short-wavelength light excitation (such as ultraviolet light, blue light or cyan light) were easily and successfully fabricated by attaching citric acid (CA) molecules onto the pristine p-CDs using an ultrasonic-triggered surface re-engineering approach under room conditions. Especially, the CA-p-CDs show truly red emission and excitation-wavelength-independent properties when excited by ultraviolet-light with a wavelength in the range of 360–400 nm or excited by progressively longer excitation wavelengths in the range of 440–500 nm. The CA-p-CDs were verified to be very low cytotoxicity. Importantly, the CA-p-CDs were further used for the targeted mitochondria imaging or direct evaluation on the intracellular biological process in living cells through variable PL emission colors with the gradually depletion of attached CA molecules. Furthermore, the strongest PL peaks of CA-p-CDs showed fluorescence enhancement response toward Cr3+ ions. The CA-p-CDs were also used for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Cr3+ ions in tap water with a limit of detection as low as 12
nM
. This work presented designable fabrication of bifunctional CDs by linking molecules as an excellent nanoprobes for the mitochondria-targetable imaging and chemical sensing of heavy metal ions in water.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have many key merits enabling them with potential applications in bioimaging, sensing, and nanomedicine, such as biocompatibility, low toxicity, small size, and ease of functionalization with specific organic molecules [1–6]. Many works have suggested that the optical properties of CDs are strongly dependent on their surface groups, attached molecules, or capped ligands [7–9]. By creating an array of molecules on the surface of CDs, the optical properties of CDs can be tuned so that a particular fluorescence emission can be associated with a specific chemical or biological process. The optical signal conversion of CDs in terms of bio-related processes, pH, redox potential, and specific ion concentrations into optical emission with specific wavelengths forms the basis of the novel array of nano molecular-based sensors. It is envisaged that the produced arrays which can then serve as part of real-time monitoring systems and thus enable multi-modal sensing. The well-designed surface of the CDs was expected to be used in the direct observation of multiple biological processes based on the quenching/restoring of emission from CDs [10,11]. Particularly, altering the surface of CDs by direct functionalization with organic molecules that target specific organelles will be an effective approach for the direct monitoring of the metabolic process of extrinsic substances.

Citric acid (CA) is an important compound in the process of converting fat, protein, and sugar into carbon dioxide in physiology [12,13]. Tricarboxylic acid cycle (TCA cycle), also called Krebs cycle or citric acid (CA) cycle, is a central driver of cellular respiration. This metabolic process usually occurs in the matrix of intracellular structures called mitochondria in most plants, animals, fungi, and many bacteria. The specific fluorescent CDs probe can be used to target mitochondria and detect intracellular silver ions [14], monitor the mitochondria process in the living cells [15], diagnosis and treatment of brain diseases [16]. Many works have been performed to attach functional extrinsic molecules to the CDs for biological applications in recent years [17–20]. Bai et al reported a novel fluorescence restoration system for the detection of DNA using CDs based on the specific binding between organic molecules and DNA [21]. Lei et al reported an efficient approach for targeted cancer cell imaging using CDs conjugated with the specific molecular receptors [18]. Li et al have demonstrated that CDs and Ag nanoparticles (NPs) composite can be used for monitoring H2O2-generated biocatalytic processes [22]. Lesani et al prepared CDs conjugation with fluorescein isothiocyanate molecules for the detection of intracellular sensing of ferric ions within the nanomolar range in fibroblasts [23]. Zhu et al showed that fluorescent CDs conjugated with H2S receptors can work as a two-photon “turn-on” platform for the selective biosensing of hydrogen sulfide in live cells and tissues [24]. However, the main obstacles in the preparation of the above-mentioned functional CDs are usually complex production processes and high cost. Furthermore, to produce highly photostable, water-dispersible, and low cytotoxicity CDs with red emission for the direct observation of biological processes in living cells remains a challenge.

Inspired by those works, we proposed that CDs containing –COOH and NH2 groups could be further surface modified using CA molecules via proper approaches to change their optical and physiological properties for specific biological applications, such as real-time membrane-penetrating and transportation of CDs in the cell, monitoring of CA consumption or conversion in the mitochondrion, or targeted imaging of organelle or ultrastructure of living cells. Owing to the biological reactions in cells, the optical properties of these well-designed CDs will be changeable. Many works also suggested that the endocytosis of CDs derived from CA into cells is much easier and faster than other CDs because of the presence of unique groups on the surface of these CDs [25,26]. More attractively, the CA can be utilized by living cells, which opened a window for the observation of CA catabolism through fluorescence “turn on” or “turn off” behaviors of CDs in living cells.

Heavy metals in the polluted water resources can enter the human body through contact or food chain, etc. The excessive heavy metals in the human body can cause a series of diseases, for example, excess Cr3+ ions can cause disorders of blood glucose levels, diabetes and obesity, and so on, so it is necessary to detect the content of metal ions [27,28]. CDs are widely used in the detection of metal ions due to their specific advantages. He et al used citric acid as the carbon source to synthesize CDs by one-step reflux method and found that CDs could detect Hg (II) ions [29]. Han et al prepared fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with a quantum yields of 42.3% by a one-step pyrolysis method using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid and urea as raw materials, it can be used to detect metal Fe3+ with a limit of detection as low as 2.6 nM [30]. The fluorescence of a new imidazole modified CDs an be significantly quenched particularly by Ni2+ in a selective manner [31].

Usually, organic macromolecules or polymeric materials have been used as modifiers for obtaining CDs with a high density of functional groups. In our approach, pristine p-CDs with orange emission were first hydrothermally synthesized using p-phenylenediamine (pPD) as precursor. Here we further report the fabrication of a fluorescence probe (CA-p-CDs) by attaching small molecules CA onto the surface of p-CDs via a sonication treatment under room temperature and room pressure. The surface charge of CA-p-CDs was tunable between positive and negative in a wide range of 11.0 mV and –22.0 mV by attaching different amounts of CA onto the purified pristine p-CDs. These CA-p-CDs were not only used for the mitochondria-targetable imaging and direct monitoring of CA metabolism in targeted mitochondria in living cells by fluorescence microscopy, but also the sensitive and selective detection of Cr3+ ions in tap water. This simple and effective approach shows a promising application of CDs after suitable modification for detecting ions in water and monitoring the metabolism process in living cells.

All these chemicals and materials were used as received without any further purification. Citric acid (CA), p-phenylenediamine (pPD), ferric chloride (FeCl3··6H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2·2H2O), ferrous chloride (FeCl2 ·4H2O), manganese chloride (MnCl2 ·4H2O), chromium chloride (CrCl3·6H2O), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2·6H2O), cupric chloride (CuCl2 ·2H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), aluminum nitrate (Al(NO3)3 ·9H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. Spectrumlabs dialysis tubing (MWCO: 1000 Da) was purchased from Morui (Shanghai) biotechnology Co., Ltd. Deionized (DI, 18 M) freshwater is produced using a Millipore water purification system (Q-Grad®1, Millipore) and used throughout all experiments.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on an Agilent Cary 60 spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by using Varian 640-IR infrared spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). DLS and Zeta potential measurements were performed using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern, U.K.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 with a field emission gun to examine the morphology and structure of CDs. The composition was analyzed using an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) purchased from EDXA, INC. Cell fluorescence image was acquired on a Nikon Eclipse CI upright fluorescence microscope and a Zeiss LSM 710 time-lapses confocal laser fluorescence microscope with × 63 oil immersion objective lens, respectively. Cells were quantified with the flow cytometer (Attune NxT, Thermo Fisher) and analyzed with FlowJo version X software (TreeStar).

The p-CDs were synthesized using pPD as the precursor following a typical and widely used hydrothermal procedure as described in the published works [32,33]. As we all know that the reaction temperature, reaction time, and concentration of precursors affect the PL peak emission due to the presence of different molecular fluorophores within and around the CDs [34,35]. The PL peak emission intensity was roughly recorded and investigated by changing reaction conditions as show in Fig. S1. As a result, p-CDs used in this work were prepared under the optimized synthesis condition. In detail, 300 mg pPD was uniformly dispersed in 60 mL deionized water. The mixture solution was loaded into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was heated up to 180 °C and kept for 14 h in a blast air oven, and then allowed to cool down to room temperature naturally. The supernatant was filtrated using BIOSHARP membrane filters with a pore size of 0.22
μ m
, the obtained transparent aqueous solution was further subjected to dialysis tubing (MWCO: 1000 Da) and dialyzed for 24 h against deionized water to remove large particles and residual reactants. Finally, the resultant solution was freeze-dried to get the purified pristine p-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Re-engineering the functional surface of p-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the decomposition, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions that occurred during the formation of p-CDs under hydrothermal conditions, the purified pristine p-CDs are rich in –NH2 and COOH groups [32,33]. Each of CA molecule has one OH group and three COOH groups, which bestowing them possibility of linkage with some inorganic nanoparticles. According to the surface groups in p-CDs and molecular structures of CA, the p-CDs can be further surface engineered with CA molecules through proper approaches to change their optical and physiological properties for specific chemical sensing or biological applications. Fortunately, CA modified p-CDs (CA-p-CDs) were easily realized under sonication treatment of mixture solution of prinstine p-CDs and CA. The general hydrothermal synthesis of p-CDs and surface re-engineering procedures for the preparation of CA-p-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . In detail, 20 mg p-CDs and 4 mg CA were dissolved in 40 mL deionized water, and then the mixture solution was ultrasonicated at atmospheric pressure and room temperature for 6 h using a Titan ultrasonic cleaner (UC-6, Output Power 180 W). After sonication treatment, the mixture solution was dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h to remove unbound CA. The control sample p-CDs but without adding CA was prepared under the same conditions.
The quantum yield (QY) of p-CDs and CA-p-CDs were determined according to the procedure introduced by Liu’s group [36].

The following Human HCC cell line Hep3B and human normal hepatic cell line LO-2 were obtained from ATCC (Rockville, MD, USA). Cells were maintained in log-phase growth in DMEM medium (Biological Industries, Israel) supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated fetal calf serum (FCS), 100 U/mL penicillin, and 0.01 mg/mL streptomycin (Biological Industries) in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Cell viability/survival assays 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was used as previously described [37,38].

For p-CDs and CA-p-CDs fluorescence analysis, human hepatocytes cells were stained with p-CDs and CA-p-CDs (10 mg/mL) for different time 5 min, 0.5 h, 1 h, 24 h, respectively. The preliminary FSC/SSC gates of the starting cell population were 10,000 events. Debris (SSC‐A vs FSC‐A) and doublets (FSC‐H vs FSC‐A) were excluded.

Human HCC cell line Hep3B and human normal hepatic cell line LO-2 were seeded on 24-well chamber slides (
4 ×
10 4
cells/well l) 24 h before the staining. Cells were fixed and washed with PBS before stained with p-CDs and CA-p-CDs for 24 h. The cells were incubated with p-CDs and CA-p-CDs in a cell incubator (37℃, 5% CO2) for 24 h. Mito-tracker Green was used to detect mitochondrial content. Mitochondrial morphology was visualized by incubating the cells with Mito-tracker Deep Green at 37 °C for 30 min. Hep3Bs were seeded and grown on glass coverslips (NEST labs, Wuxi, Jiangsu, China). Cellular imaging was analyzed using fluorescence microscopy and confocal laser fluorescence microscopy after being rinsed with PBS again. Cells were quantified by the flow cytometer. All the excitation wavelengths were set within the range of 510–530, 460–480, and 360–380 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation of p-CDs and CA-p-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Standard MTT method was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of p-CDs and CA-p-CDs. The human HCC cell line Hep3B and human normal hepatic cell line LO-2 were used for the cytotoxicity evaluation. Hep3B cells and LO-2 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of
5 ×
10 3
cells
per well and cultured in a culture medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/ streptomycin in a 37 ℃ incubator with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, the Hep3B cells and LO-2 cells were treated with p-CDs and CA-p-CDs at concentration of 25, 50, 100, 250, 500
μ g
/ mL
, respectively. Five replicate wells in each group were used for every control and tested concentrations. The plate after p-CDs and CA-p-CDs treatments was then incubated at
37
℃for another 24 h. The culture medium was discarded after incubation. Afterward, 100
μ L
MTT was added into each well and incubated with cells for an additional 4 h. Then, the culture medium was removed and replaced with 100
μ L
DMSO per well, and the absorbance was measured at the wavelength in the range of 490–550 nm using Thermo Scientific Multiskan MK3 microplate reader. The cell viability was determined as the relative percentage of non-treated cells by the following equation:
cell viability

490 , c o n t r o l

490 , c o n t r o l

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and composition of p-CDs and CA-p-CDs.
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of the p-CDs and CA-p-CDs were observed by TEM. As seen from Fig. 2 a and b, the morphology and size distribution of CA-p-CDs shows the particle is much larger and more uniform after surface re-engineering compared with that of p-CDs. The average size of p-CDs is about 4.4 nm, but for CA-p-CDs is about 7.9 nm. The particles in p-CDs were well separated from each other, whilst those in CA-p-CDs are aggregated together. Such aggregation might be attributed to the surface attachment of CA molecules and further linkage between CA-p-CDs.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed to identify the chemical bonds and functional groups on p-CDs and CA-p-CDs. Based on the molecular structures of CA, CA has one OH group and three COOH groups. It can be found from the FT-IR spectrum of CA in Fig. 3 that several typical characteristic peaks including the –COOH stretching (wide shoulder peaks at 3010 cm−1, 2572 cm−1, and 1739 cm−1, respectively), the band at 1415 cm−1 and 1209 cm−1 belong to the C–C, C-OH stretching vibration. For the p-CDs, the bands at 3340 cm−1 and 3208 cm−1 belong to the O–H, N–H, and C–H stretching vibration, the bands at 1612 cm−1 and 1515 cm−1 belong to the C=O, benzene stretching vibration [39,40]. These results indicate that –COOH, O–H, N–H, and benzene may exist on the surface of p-CDs, which can not only improve their hydrophilicity and stability, but also provide the possibility of surface modification with a suitable ligand. For the CA-p-CDs, the bands at 3322 cm−1 and 3205 cm−1 belong to the O–H, N–H, and C–H stretching vibration, the bands at 1635 cm−1 and 1519 cm−1 belong to the C=O, benzene stretching vibration, and 1290 cm−1 belong to the C-OH Stretching vibration. And the shoulder peak at 3322 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1 belong to the vibration of carbonyl amide groups [41]. In addition, Fig. 3 also shows that the central peak position of the C=O stretching is red shifted from 1612 cm−1 to 1635 cm−1 after the sonication treatment of p-CDs and CA.

Fig. 4 shows the XPS spectra of p-CDs and CA-p-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4a, three peaks are identified in the full scan spectra, implying that p-CDs and CA-p-CDs have the same elemental composition. The contents of C, N, O elements for p-CDs are 75.84%, 15.14%, and 9.02%, for CA-p-CDs are 72.15%, 17.41%, and 10.43%, respectively. It should be noted that content of C and O element in CA is about 37.5% and 58.3%, respectively. The element content change implies the attachment of CA onto the p-CDs. The further analysis on high-resolution N1s spectrum of p-CDs reveals that N element present in three different chemical environments, ascribing to C–N at 397.9 eV, C–N–C at 398.6 eV and N–H at 399.9 eV, whilst CA-p-CDs have C–N at 398.3 eV, C–N–C at 399.3 eV, O=C–NH at 399.8 eV and N–H at 401.0 eV. Moreover, the corresponding high-resolution N1s peak position of CA-p-CDs exhibits a shift of 0.6 eV toward higher binding energy after surface re-engineering, which implying the charge transfer from nitrogen to electron acceptor in CONH bond, a more electronegative bond originates from the reaction between COOH and NH. XPS results also imply that the content and chemical environment of dominant elements have changed after sonication treatment due to the combination of p-CDs and CA.

Usually, the extrinsic molecules can be directly and easily physically adsorbed on CDs through interaction such as
π - π
stacking, and van der Waals force. The interaction between p-CDs with NH2 groups and CA with COOH groups would result in the formation of O=C–NH groups, resulting in the CA-p-CDs with higher N content, which has been confirmed by the XPS results. Furthermore, the vibration of O=C–NH bonds in FT-IR are identified, which is consistent with XPS results. These results demonstrate that CA-p-CDs were successfully surface re-engineered through conjugating p-CDs with CA molecules after simple sonication treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of p-CDs and CA-p-CDs.
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 a, the normalized photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra of CA-p-CDs and p-CDs have two excitation peaks, one is narrow and symmetrical excitation peak centered at around 365 nm, another is broad and asymmetrical peak in the range of 465–480 nm. However, the PLE spectrum of CA-p-CDs show noticeable change compared with p-CDs. The PLE peaks of CA-p-CDs were narrowest and more symmetrical than that of p-CDs. The first peak is originated from the CDs’ dominant energy state, the second one is originated from functional groups on the surface of p-CDs and CA-p-CDs. A new and well aligned functional amide group is formed on the surface of CA-p-CDs under sonication treatment. Thus, a symmetrical blue-shifted PLE peak and adsorption peak of CA-p-CDs were observed in Fig. 5(a) and (b), respectively. Dual- emission CDs have been synthesized and applied for the visual assay of Pb2+ ions [42], bioimaging [43,44]. As far as we know, two-excitation and single red-emission CDs have not been reported. UV–vis absorption spectra of both p-CDs and CA-p-CDs in Fig. 5b display higher absorption shoulder and lower energy absorption peak at 275 nm, and 516 nm, respectively. The absorption shoulder at 275 nm is attributed to the π → π ∗ transition of the conjugated C=C bonds and aromatic sp2 domain, while the absorption peak at 516 nm is ascribed to the n → π ∗ transition of the C=O band. The pure CA solution has no absorption at the same peak position [45]. It should be noted that the absorption of CA-p-CDs was obviously increased after sonication treatment for surface modification at the same concentration, which might be attributed to surface re-engineering of p-CDs from the combination of CA molecules with –COOH, O–H, and N–H groups.

As shown in Fig. 5c and d, the strongest PL spectra of both p-CDs and CA-p-CDs with a concentration of
125 μ g / m L
are centered at 610 and 650 nm in an aqueous solution when excited by ultraviolet-light with progressively longer excitation wavelengths from 360 nm to 400 nm in 10 nm increment, respectively. Correspondingly, the p-CDs and CA-p-CDs emit orange and red fluorescence under UV light irradiation, respectively, as seen in the inset of Fig. 5c and d.

As shown in Fig. S2, the p-CDs and CA-p-CDs still emit the same visible light under longer excitation wavelengths from 430 nm to 500 nm in 10 nm increment. A hundredfold PL intensity of CA-p-CDs is lower than that of p-CDs, which can be attributed to the change of surface state due to the linkage between surface groups of p-CDs and CA as well as the subsequent attachment of CA molecules onto the surface of p-CDs. The PL peak position of CA-p-CDs also exhibits a significant shift to the red-light region compared with that of p-CDs. Along with the red-shift in PL peak, a significant decrease in emission intensity was also recorded. In particular, the PL spectra of CA-p-CDs were more excitation-wavelength-independent than p-CDs when excited by ultraviolet-light or visible light, which might be attributed to the uniform distribution of radiative recombination sites on the surface re-engineered CA-p-CDs. The PL spectra of p-CDs show concentration-independent properties with the increasing of concentration in a wide range under optimal excitation wavelengths, in contrast, CA-p-CDs show concentration-dependent behaviors as shown in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4. When the concentration of CA-p-CDs is higher than 50
μ g
/
mL
as shown in Fig. S3(b), a red shift in the emission wavelength is identified under the same excitation wavelength. Fig. S4 clearly demonstrates the dependence of normalized PL emission of p-CDs and CA-p-CD on concentration. The fluorescence emission intensity continuously decreases with the increasing concentration of CA-p-CDs could be attributed to the collisional quenching and self-absorption quenching in high-concentration solutions.

The above results implied that the modification of p-CDs using CA did affect the optical performance of obtained CA-p-CDs. The CA-p-CDs prepared by ultrasonic triggered surface re-engineering present the change of surface state, which leads to the decrease of fluorophore and red-shift of strongest PL peak position. As proved by Duan et al [46] and Hu et al’s [47] works, these biocompatible CDs functionalized by or attached with extraneous CA molecules could be used in biological species for direct observation of biological reactions or processes. More importantly, CDs with near-infrared emission are ideal candidates for fluorescence imaging and photothermal cancer therapy [48,49]. According to the synthetic mechanism of CDs using CA molecules as carbon resources, the direct pyrolysis or decomposition of CA has no PL emission [47]. Thus, the CA molecules themselves after sonication treatment cannot shift peak the PL peak position or compromising the peak emission intensity directly, the change of PL properties of CA-p-CDs might be attributed to the attachment of CA molecules to p-CDs surface. As shown in Table S1, the QY of p-CDs and CA-p-CDs is quite stable under different excitation wavelength, but the the QY of surface re-engineered CA-p-CDs decreased greatly in comparison with p-CDs under different excitation wavelength. A great drop of QY as well as a red-shift of their PL emission implies that there should be more complicated electronic structures in CA-p-CDs due to the formation of specific interface and surface structure.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the zeta potential of pure CA solution was measured to be –22.5 mV. The zeta potential of p-CDs was 12.6 mV in deionized water on account of plentiful functional –COOH and NH2 groups on their surface, which implying that purified pristine p-CDs are positively charged. Naturally, the zeta potential of surface-modified CA-p-CDs was −17.5 mV due to the presence of new emerged O=C–NH groups [50,51]. These results imply that the surface potential of CA-p-CDs can be controlled with the amount of CA molecules attached to the surface of p-CDs. The CA molecules on the surface of CA-p-CDs endow them with excellent bio-compatibility and hydrophilicity. Notably, the CA-p-CDs modified with CA molecules can easily enter into cells or be organelle targeting transported via endocytosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation of the p-CDs and CA-p-CDs.
ce:section.ce:para:
In vitro MTT assay was used to test the toxicities of p-CDs and CA-p-CDs to human HCC cell line Hep3B and human normal hepatic cell line LO2. As we expected, the cell viability of LO2 cells and Hep3B cells is quite stable and good in the whole stands, no significant difference was observed with the increasing dose of H2O as shown in Fig. 7 a and c. By contrast, the cell viability increased significantly when LO2 cells and Hep3B cells were exposed to CA-p-CDs with a concentration higher than 250
μ g
/ mL
as shown in Fig. 7b and d. The results not only imply the excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of p-CDs, but also display the capability of CA-p-CDs in promoting proliferation and growth of cells. As we described before, the high cell viability of LO2 cells and Hep3B cells incubated with CA-p-CDs might attribute to the targeted transportation of CA to the mitochondrion and further consumption or conversion in it to provide the living cells with energy for their growth and division.

Therefore, we envisaged that CA-p-CDs are capable of targeting and labeling intracellular structure-mitochondria, more importantly, consumption or conversion of attached CA molecules on the surface of CA-p-CDs may provide a window for the direct observation of biological processes in living cells through the alteration of fluorescence.

To further determine the capability of CA-p-CDs in specific targeting and selectively imaging of mitochondria, a commercially available mitochondria-specific staining fluorescent dye, i.e., Mito-tracker Green, is employed to co-stain Hep3B cells with the p-CDs and CA-p-CDs, respectively. After Hep3B cells being incubated with the p-CDs, CA-p-CDs, Mito-tracker Green, and two blends of Mito-tracker Green with p-CDs or CA-p-CDs, respectively, cellular fluorescence images of Hep3B cells were firstly performed by a fluorescence microscope as shown in Fig. S5. To discriminate the relative location of mitochondria in Hep3B cells, the nucleus dye 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) is also used to stain the nucleus of these cells. As shown in Fig. S5a, green or red fluorescence spots was observed under excitation at 552 nm and 633 nm, respectively. The merged images roughly suggest that P-CDs primarily stain cytoplasm, but CA-p-CDs stain mitochondria. Furthermore, the cellular fluorescence images of Hep3B cells stained by Mito, Mito-tracker Green with p-CDs or CA-p-CDs also reveal that CA-p-CDs mainly distribute in mitochondria. It is encouraging that the emission remained in cells after 24 h.

Next, to visualize the change of fluorescence emission intensity profile of CA-p-CDs in cell metabolism process, flow cytometry analysis was also performed at 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, and 24 h after cell staining. As shown in Fig. 8 , we found that the fluorescence intensity of CA-p-CDs increases over time, which could be attributed to the consumption of CA and the continuous increasing concentration of CA-p-CDs in the Hep3B cells. These results implied that CA-p-CDs can be used for the real-time monitoring of the metabolic process of extrinsic CA in the living cells.

The confocal fluorescence images of Hep3B cells stained by the CA-p-CDs and Mito-tracker Green are shown in Fig. 9 . It was found that the observed green fluorescence from Mito-tracker Green almost overlaps with the red fluorescence from CA-p-CDs. The capability of specifically staining the mitochondria of these surface functionalized CA-p-CDs is mainly attributed to the preferential affinity between CA and mitochondria. As seen from the merged image, the region of red fluorescence emission in Hep3B cells matches well with that of the green fluorescence emission from Mito-tracker Green, demonstrating that the CA-p-CDs primarily distributes in mitochondria. The results obviously verify that the CA-p-CDs exhibit quite well mitochondria-targetable property, which is crucial for the further design and preparation of mitochondria targeted drug delivery system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Cr3+ in tap water using the CA-p-CDs.
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of p-CDs and CA-p-CDs was investigated by adding different metal ions with the same concentration. As shown in Fig. S6, the strongest PL peak intensity of p-CDs decreases by 53% and 23% upon addition of Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively, but instead of increasing by 193% upon addition of Cr3+. But other metal ions such as Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, can hardly influence the PL emission. The selectivity results of p-CDs indicate that Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+ may interfere seriously with each other, which is one of the most important limiting factors for their further application in the real sample. This interference could be ascribed to the complicated surface groups of p-CDs. Thus, it is expected that further modification of p-CDs for creating a uniform and homogeneous surface state is the key to resolve this problem.

After surface attachment of p-CDs using CA molecules, we found that the strongest PL peak intensity of CA-p-CDs increase by 203% and 17% upon addition of Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions, respectively, while other metal ion shows negligible quenching or enhancing effect as shown in Fig. 10 a, Thus, it shows that the CA-p-CDs have satisfying selectivity for Cr3+ ions assay. It is proposed that the carboxylic group and hydroxyl group on the surface of CA-p-CDs interacted with Cr3+, leading to the consequent alterations of electronic structure in CA-p-CDs. Generally, the photoinduced electron transfer from from electron-rich groups to electron-lack unit resulted in the fluorescence quenching. A probable mechanism of fluorescence enhancement of p-CDs and CA-p-CDs upon addition of Cr3+ ions is ascribed to the photoinduced charge transfer and restrained exciton recombination. The existence of benzene ring and lack electron body in p-CDs and CA-p-CDs was proven by the FT-IR and XPS spectra. Therefore, the electron transfer from the benzene ring to lack electron body that favored nanoradiative decay is expected to be inhibited or hindered with the addition of Cr3+ ions. Thus, the emission of CA-p-CDs was greatly enhanced with the addition of Cr3+ ions.

The applicability of CA-p-CDs for the detection of Cr3+ in the real samples was investigated. A standard addition method was established to detect the concentration of Cr3+ in the sample of tap water. Usually, tap water is clear from Cr3+ ions. The tap water sample was directly used to detect the presence of Cr3+ions. Apparently, the results showed no Cr3+ ion was detected in the tap water from Jinghai District, Tianjin, China, which is consistent with the water quality announcement by Tianjin Water Group. Therefore, different concentrations of Cr3+ (0–200
nM
) water samples were separately spiked into 3.0 mL CA-p-CDs aqueous solution with a concentration of
50 μ g
/ mL
.

The results showed that the PL peak intensity of CA-p-CDs aqueous solution increases with the concentration of Cr3+ ions in a wide range of 0–200
nM
as shown in Fig. 10b. The surface re-engineered CA-p-CDs was used for the detection of heavy metals in tap water and showed a good selectivity and sensitivity towards Cr3+ with a LOD as low as 12
nM
, and the maximum concentration of Cr3+ in drinking water is defined by WHO and China as 0.96
μ M
[58]. In order to show the advantages of the developed method, the comparison with the reported method was summarized and listed in Table 1 . The results suggested that CA-p-CDs probe had a lower LOD toward Cr3+ ions compared to the reported works. Conclusively, the CA-p-CDs probe is a simple and practical probe, which attributes to designing and developing more efficient and economic fluorescence chemosensors.

In conclusion, a bifunctional and biocompatible CA-p-CDs nanoprobes for the mitochondria-targetable imaging and detection of Cr3+ ions in tap water are prepared via an ultrasonic triggered surface-engineering approach. These biocompatible CA-p-CDs show rapid endocytosis rate and mitochondrial targeting ability. The PL peak position of CA-p-CDs shift to red region with the consumption of surface attached CA in the living cell, which make them are capable for the further application in dynamic monitoring of the intracellular biological process and design of mitochondrial targeting drug delivery system. The CA-p-CDs were also used for the highly selective and sensitive detection of Cr3+ ions in tap water. Different from most other CDs, the interaction between Cr3+ ions and CA-p-CDs leads to the gradual enhancement of PL intensity with the concentration increasing of extrinsic ions. The enhancement of PL intensity might be attributed to the blocking of the photoinduced electron transfer process for the continuous surface-chelating interaction between Cr3+ ions and CDs. This work clearly shows that CA-p-CDs can both serve as a promising nanocarrier for preparing other mitochondrial-targetable nanomedicine through linking different drug molecular, but also paves an avenue for the surface re-engineering of CDs for the selective and sensitive detection of metal ions.
